content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
P.O. Box 807 • 428 J Street • Sacramento, CA 95812-0807
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

December 11, 2001

John Brown
500 Esplanade Drive, Suite 1270
Oxnard, CA 93030

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-01-276

Dear Mr. Brown:

This is in response to your letter requesting advice on behalf of Robert W.
Wilson, the proponent of a state ballot measure, regarding the campaign provisions of the
Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹

QUESTION

May Mr. Wilson solicit contributions by electronic mail ("email")? Do the mass
mailing identification requirements of the Act apply to email communications?

CONCLUSION

There is nothing in the Act that would prohibit Mr. Wilson from soliciting
contributions for a ballot measure committee via email. Neither is there any
identification requirement imposed by the Act on email communications.

FACTS

Robert W. Wilson is the proponent of a proposed state constitutional initiative,
currently in Summary and Title with the State Attorney General's office. Mr. Wilson
proposes to make a solicitation for campaign contributions by email.

_______________________
¹ Government Code sections 81000 – 91014. Commission regulations appear at title 2, sections
18109 - 18997, of the California Code of Regulations.

December 11, 2001
Page Two

ANALYSIS

The Act requires that ballot measure committees disclose and maintain records for
contributions received, but does not otherwise regulate solicitation of campaign
contributions by email. Additionally, other than sender identification requirements, the
Act does not regulate mass mailings that solicit contributions.

In regard to sender identification, Section 84305 states, in part:

"...no candidate or committee shall send a mass mailing unless
the name, street address, and city of the candidate or committee
are shown on the outside of each piece of mail..."

Section 82041.5 states that:

"'Mass mailing' means over two hundred substantially similar
pieces of mail, but does not include a form letter or other mail
which is sent in response to an unsolicited request, letter or
other inquiry."

In Vines Advice Letter, No. I-95-275, the Commission stated:

"...the requirement to print any identifying information on materials
distributed by a candidate or committee is applicable only to materials
sent through the mail."

Electronic mailings, therefore, are not currently regulated by Section 84305.

In your letter, you specifically asked if this sort of mailing would "violate any
California elections laws." Please note that we do not interpret the California Elections
Code or any statute other than the Political Reform Act. You may contact the Secretary
of State's Elections Division at (916) 657-2166 for any questions pertaining to the
Elections Code.

December 11, 2001
Page Three

If you have any further questions, please feel free to contact me toll-free at (866)
275-3772.

Sincerely,

Luisa Menchaca
General Counsel

By: Kevin S. Moen, PhD
Political Reform Consultant II
